channel.title: The Frontier Brief
channel.description: Signals, synthesis, and thinking at the intersection of AI, product leadership, and enterprise fintech.

item.title: We Solved the Speed Problem. We Created a Clarity Problem.
item.author: Thomas Lukoma
item.description: AI made building faster. That should mean more learning, more testing, more iteration. Instead, most teams are producing more noise, faster. The problem isn't capability. It's clarity.

item.title: Your AI Agent Can Read Your Codebase. It Doesn't Know Your Product.
item.author: Fabricio Teixeira

description.p: Thomas's lens: Context engineering is emerging as the critical skill gap between generic AI-assisted development and distinctive product building. As AI agents become standard tooling, competitive advantage shifts from what the agent can do to what product knowledge you can articulate and systematize. This is a new product leadership capability: capturing the 60% of product identity that doesn't live in code.

blockquote.p: Code carries maybe 40% of it, enough to infer tokens, layout conventions, entity names, API shapes. The other 60% lives outside the code: in design files, in marketing copy, in decisions. An agent that only reads code will make up the rest.

item.title: On the Ring of Power and AGI Dynamics
item.author: Sam Altman

description.p: Thomas's lens: This reveals the extreme pressure and polarization facing AI platform leaders. For product builders, Altman's framing suggests the strategic imperative isn't just technical excellence but designing for distributed access and democratic accountability from the start—concentrated control models will face escalating resistance.

blockquote.p: Once you see AGI you can't unsee it. It has a real 'ring of power' dynamic to it, and makes people do crazy things. I don't mean that AGI is the ring itself, but instead the totalizing philosophy of 'being the one to control AGI'.

item.title: Vibe Coding in Prod: Delegating to AI Without Loss of Control
item.author: Eric

description.p: Thomas's lens: As AI capability doubles every 7 months, line-by-line code review becomes a bottleneck. Eric's framework for "vibe coding responsibly"—fully delegating large tasks to Claude while verifying via tests, not inspection—flips the script on how teams scale. The key insight: treat AI like a junior hire (invest upfront in context/requirements), focus AI on leaf nodes (low-dependency features), keep humans owning architecture, and design for verifiability (tests + observable behavior) rather than code inspection.

blockquote.p: Computer RAM went from kilobytes to terabytes—a million-fold improvement. AI will go from 'help with one function' to 'rewrite entire systems.' Karpathy's point about early compilers being distrusted but nobody reading assembly now predicts painful irrelevance for engineers who demand to write every line.

item.title: The Shape of the Thing: From Working With AI to Managing AI
item.author: Ethan Mollick

description.p: Thomas's lens: The shift from 'working with AI' to 'managing AI' requires rethinking product team structure, staffing ratios, and what human roles should focus on. If engineers can delegate $1000/day in AI work (equivalent to their salary), traditional headcount-based capacity planning becomes obsolete—velocity is now constrained by oversight capacity and strategic direction, not coding bandwidth.

blockquote.p: Starting in late 2025, we entered a new era thanks to AI agents like Claude Code, OpenAI's Codex, and OpenClaw. These are AI systems that you can just give work to, sometimes hours of human work, and get back reasonable and useful results in minutes. This is an era of managing AIs, rather than working with them.

item.title: Product Roadmaps: How the Best Product Teams Plan for Uncertainty
item.author: Teresa Torres

description.p: Thomas's lens: For product leaders, this directly addresses the tension between rapid experimentation (essential for discovering what actually works) and organizational coordination. Outcome-based roadmaps become critical for maintaining stakeholder trust while preserving team autonomy to pivot when needed.

blockquote.p: Feature-based roadmaps are fiction. Everyone on the product team knows it. The challenge is the rest of the organization sees it as a contract. When the product team breaks the contract, as they inevitably will do, trust erodes.

item.title: A Guide to Which AI to Use in the Agentic Era
item.author: Ethan Mollick

description.p: Thomas's lens: This decomposition of AI products into models/apps/harnesses is the new competitive landscape. Product differentiation increasingly comes from harness design (what tools you give the AI, how you orchestrate multi-step workflows) rather than model quality alone, since frontier models are converging in capability. Your moat isn't exclusive access to smart AI—it's how you instrument it to deliver value.

blockquote.p: Until recently, you didn't have to know this. The model was the product, the app was the website, and the harness was minimal. You typed, it responded, you typed again. Now the same model can behave very differently depending on what harness it's operating in.

item.title: Why I Released an AI Agent That Scared Everyone | Peter Schönemann
item.author: peter-schonemann-openclaw-agents

description.p: Thomas's lens: Agents improvise and persist; chatbots fail gracefully. The shift from instruction-following to emergent autonomy redefines product development.

blockquote.p: The bottleneck is no longer typing. It's thinking. And thinking was the part I did 25 years. Building software felt like playing a video game again.

item.title: Why half of product managers are in trouble | Nikhyl Singhal (Meta, Google)
item.author: Lenny Rachitsky

description.p: Thomas's lens: The information mover is becoming obsolete. PMs who've built careers on moving data up/down org hierarchies will struggle. The split is now binary: builders win big, non-builders are in trouble. But for builders, the constraints have lifted—you can ship, test, and validate directly. The real skill now is judgment at speed.

blockquote.p: The information mover is essentially going to become a dinosaur... if you don't love building stuff, you're in trouble. Builders are going to have the time of their lives.

item.title: Changes in the system prompt between Claude Opus 4.6 and 4.7
item.author: Simon Willison

description.p: Thomas's lens: This reveals how production AI systems balance proactivity with safety at scale—the shift to "make a reasonable attempt now, not interview first" represents a fundamental UX philosophy that could inform your own AI product design. The toolsearch pattern shows how to handle capability discovery gracefully rather than falsely claiming "I can't do that." Understanding these prompt engineering patterns gives you a blueprint for building AI products that feel helpful without being pushy or overly cau

blockquote.p: When a request leaves minor details unspecified, the person typically wants Claude to make a reasonable attempt now, not to be interviewed first.

item.title: Build to Learn vs Build to Earn
item.author: Marty Cagan

description.p: Thomas's lens: A foundational tension in any roadmap, made sharper by AI. When AI can build faster, the cost of 'build to learn' drops — which means teams that still treat every build like a revenue bet are leaving discovery value on the table.

blockquote.p: The distinction between building to learn and building to earn is one of the most important and least understood in product work.

item.title: Building Todoist Ramble: How Doist Turned Voice Braindumps into Real-Time Task Capture
item.author: Teresa Torres

description.p: Thomas's lens: A clean case study of AI-native product thinking: start with a real friction point (capture during motion), use AI to solve the right part of the problem (parsing intent from voice), ship small. The pattern matters more than the specific feature.

blockquote.p: Continuous discovery isn't about running more research — it's about building faster feedback loops into how you work.

item.title: Not all AI agents are created equal
item.author: Lenny Rachitsky

description.p: Thomas's lens: Most teams try to prioritize fundamentally different kinds of agents as if they were the same thing — using impact/effort matrices built for features, not systems. The real unlock: categorize by agent type first, then prioritize within type.

blockquote.p: The problem isn't that they lack ideas; it's that they try to prioritize fundamentally different kinds of systems as if they were the same thing.

item.title: Product Work Is Relationship Work - All Things Product Podcast with Teresa Torres & Petra Wille
item.author: Teresa Torres

description.p: Thomas's lens: As AI agents handle more tactical product work, the differentiated value of product leaders shifts entirely to the interpersonal layer—stakeholder alignment, building shared context, and navigating organizational dynamics. Product leaders who treat collaboration as transactional will become bottlenecks; those who invest in relational work and curiosity will unlock faster execution because teams operate from shared understanding rather than constant negotiation. This reframes "product politics" f

blockquote.p: While AI can help build things faster, it can't replace the relationship work required to align stakeholders, navigate competing priorities, and create shared understanding across teams.

item.title: Vibe coding is accelerating the erosion of design authority
item.author: Fabricio Teixeira

description.p: Thomas's lens: The excitement is real but the gap is also real: 'something that looks finished' is not 'something that is finished.' As someone who bridges UX and front-end, this is both a threat (design devalued) and an opportunity (be the person who closes the gap).

blockquote.p: The excitement surrounding these tools does not come from the design itself. It comes from the collapse of the distance between description and interaction.

item.title: Commercial vs Internal Products
item.author: Marty Cagan

description.p: Thomas's lens: For AI-native product leaders, this distinction is crucial because AI is simultaneously lowering the barrier to create solutions while raising the competitive bar for commercial products. The speed of AI-driven competition means product discovery skills are now existential—you can't just build a working AI feature, you must build one so compelling it drives switching behavior in an increasingly crowded market.

blockquote.p: It is next level hard to take ownership of an outcome for a commercial product, which has to win against a rapidly growing list of competitors before it has the chance to deliver real business results.

item.title: 🎙️ This week on How I AI: I gave Claude Code our entire codebase. Our customers noticed.
item.author: Lenny Rachitsky

description.p: Thomas's lens: This demonstrates a strategic shift from using AI for product velocity to using it for service differentiation—custom technical support that accounts for each customer's specific infrastructure is harder to replicate than product features. It reveals that AI can fundamentally restructure the technical knowledge boundary between customer-facing teams and engineering, enabling field teams to answer questions previously requiring deep codebase expertise. The pattern of treating code as the primary

blockquote.p: Compete on customer experience, not just product velocity. Everyone uses AI to ship faster products. Al uses AI to show up differently in customer relationships—delivering custom deployment documentation that accounts for each customer's specific security requirements and infrastructure constraints.

item.title: Claude Dispatch and the Power of Interfaces
item.author: Ethan Mollick

description.p: Thomas's lens: The chatbot interface creates cognitive overhead that erodes the productivity gain of AI. This is a UX problem masquerading as an AI capability problem — and it's wide open. The PMs who see this clearly will build the right interfaces.

blockquote.p: The AI presented information in a way that completely overwhelmed people: giant walls of text, offers to pursue new topics, and sprawling discussions. The chatbot interface appeared to be the obstacle, not the work.

item.title: Why Product Sense is the only product skill that will matter in the AI age
item.author: Shreyas Doshi

description.p: Thomas's lens: AI tools commoditize execution. What remains irreplaceable is judgment: reading customers, markets, and tradeoffs better than the AI can. For someone repositioning their career, this is the bet to make — not on tool fluency, but on sense.

blockquote.p: Tools have never been a significant source of alpha in product success and that is not changing with AI tools.

item.title: Product Coaching and AI
item.author: Marty Cagan

description.p: Thomas's lens: This exposes a defining trap for product managers: using AI to accelerate "project model" work (roadmaps, PRDs, aggregating feedback) reveals you're doing theater that engineers or agents could do themselves. The strategic opportunity is using AI to develop genuine product sense and learn the product model—distinguishing value-creating PMs from those at risk of replacement. You must explicitly instruct your AI coach which operating model to prioritize or you'll get confused, generic advice.

blockquote.p: If you're just using AI in order to accelerate the project model by showing how trivial it is to aggregate requests and feedback, generate roadmaps, and create PRD's and/or user stories, realize that an agent – or your engineer or designer – could just as easily do this themselves.
